ranked_opportunities.scqa: The SAR Buyer Side
ranked_opportunities.verdict: The originally listed 'Business Development Manager, Space Services' role (scored 70.5) is no longer advertised: its posting returns HTTP 410 Gone as of June 21, 2026, confirming Paul's read. The closest live successor is 'Business Development Manager, Satellite Sales', open in three US variants (remote-flexible anchored in Washington DC, remote-flexible anchored in Los Angeles, and on-site in Denver), all at a disclosed $140,000 to $210,000 plus uncapped commission. It is an individual-contributor territory seat: own US territory from prospecting to close across commercial satellite, components, and mission services. The named bar, 5 to 10 years of satellite, space, or aerospace BD, Paul clears cleanly from his KSAT satellite-services decade and GFW satellite-data commercial work, so no Hard Requirement Cap fires and there are no hard blockers (US-authorized; only a soft relocation flag for the anchor city and 30 to 40 percent travel). It lands a few points under the dead Space Services seat because Satellite Sales is a broader, more transactional territory role than the services-led 'first BDM' build that made the original a 70.5: SA 7 (US new-customer-acquisition is the central thrust, but at territory level), SPM 5 (EnduroSat's SAR and shared-mission situation is pattern-adjacent, but this seat is conventional territory sales), RF, IL, CO, GC all 7. Net 67.0, Tier 2, pursue with caveats. The 2026-05-15 company deep dive (Rusty Thomas US build, $147M raised, MetaSensing X-band SAR partnership) stays valid; only the role and its SCQA angle changed.
ranked_opportunities.doc_note: Company deep dive completed 2026-05-15 (Rusty Thomas US build, $147M, MetaSensing SAR). The deep dive's specific role and top SCQA ('The SAR Buyer Side') were written for the now-closed Space Services seat; the company analysis still holds but the role-specific framing needs a refresh for Satellite Sales.

ranked_opportunities.scqa: Precedent Before the Prime
ranked_opportunities.verdict: Full deep dive 2026-07-06. Orbit Fab is a post-demo, pre-commercial-revenue space-infrastructure company at a founder-transition inflection (CEO Faber out March 2026, no permanent CEO, 20M Series B), converting years of government-funded refueling demos into first commercial dollars. The strategic problem, making its RAFTI port the commercial default before Northrop's co-endorsed PRM interface locks in, maps almost exactly onto Paul's pilot-to-precedent pattern (SPM 8) and the commercialization inflection is his KSAT sweet spot (SA 7); but the JD's 'proven capture and proposal management' requirement is a real gap that soft-caps RF at 5 and IL at 4. Net 57.5, Tier 3 stretch. Clearance NOT required (clears the defense line). Pursue with caveats: high personal interest, but eyes open on the Colorado-hybrid relocation, the formal-capture gap, and the company's operational instability.
ranked_opportunities.doc_note: Full deep dive completed 2026-07-06
ranked_opportunities.hook: RAFTI-as-industry-standard is Paul's write-the-regulation pattern almost literally, at a commercialization inflection; the gaps are formal government capture and a Colorado-hybrid relocation.

blockers.detail: Colorado-based hybrid (Lafayette, CO office plus remote); the posting says all roles are based in Colorado. Not US-remote, so a Taos base means relocating to the Denver-Boulder area. Paul is open to relocation.

JD requires 'Proven experience leading proposal development and capture management processes.' Paul has built complex commercial and partnership deals but has not run formal government capture/proposal management. Adjacent not direct; would pair with a capture specialist. Soft-caps RF at 5, IL at 4.

ranked_opportunities.role: Commercial Development Manager @ Foundation Alloy Technology Explorations, Inc
ranked_opportunities.verdict: Foundation Alloy is commercializing a novel metallurgy platform into aerospace, defense, and energy markets, domains adjacent to Paul's satellite and maritime work. However, the role is a mid-level support position ("Support business development," "Coordinate customer proposals," "Manage outbound customer engagement activities") requiring only 4+ years of experience, not the strategic market-building leadership Paul typically pursues. The JD's "strongly preferred" requirement for "experience in advanced manufacturing, metallurgy, advanced materials" is a soft gap: Paul's 15 years in satellite services and EO data are adjacent to aerospace/defense but not the metallurgy/materials manufacturing domain. The company is already deploying products and scaling infrastructure, so the role involves some new-build work (pilots, new customer segments) but within an existing commercial structure. This is a competent role at an interesting company, but the seniority, sector depth, and role shape are all stretches for Paul.

blockers.detail: Role is titled 'Commercial Development Manager' but appears to be a mid-level IC support role (4+ years experience requirement) rather than the strategic market-building leadership position Paul typically pursues. Job verbs are 'support,' 'coordinate,' 'manage outbound,' suggesting execution rather than strategy ownership.

JD states 'Interest or experience in advanced manufacturing, metallurgy, advanced materials, aerospace, defense, robotics, semiconductors, or related industrial technologies is strongly preferred.' Paul's 15 years span satellite operations, EO data services, and maritime surveillance, adjacent to aerospace/defense but not metallurgy or advanced materials manufacturing.

ranked_opportunities.role: Senior Director, Subscription & Data Solutions @ LevelTen Energy | Techstars Job Board
ranked_opportunities.verdict: This role requires deep domain experience in the renewable energy PPA market that Paul does not possess. The JD explicitly requires "10+ years of experience in the renewable energy market, with deep knowledge of PPAs, energy procurement, and the buyer/seller/advisor/developer ecosystem" and "Significant commercial or sales experience within this market, you have personally sold to or managed relationships with energy buyers, developers, utilities, or advisors." Paul's 15 years of commercial infrastructure experience is in satellite monitoring (oil & gas, fishing surveillance, maritime), not renewable energy transactions. While the role structure, commercial-product bridge, subscription packaging, data monetization strategy, maps beautifully to Paul's skills from GFW (building commercial licensing for an open platform, multi-stakeholder deal architecture), the sector mismatch is a hard blocker. RF capped at 5 because the responsibilities fit Paul's profile but the required domain does not. IL capped at 4 because Paul cannot credibly claim the named renewable energy market experience or customer base. The role is well-compensated and the stage is interesting (post-PMF data platform scaling subscriptions), but Paul would need to explain a full sector pivot from space/maritime to renewable energy, and the JD signals they want someone who has "been living and breathing this market for years."

blockers.type: Hard requirement: 10+ years renewable energy market experience
blockers.detail: JD requires '10+ years of experience in the renewable energy market, with deep knowledge of PPAs, energy procurement, and the buyer/seller/advisor/developer ecosystem.' Paul has 15 years in commercial space/satellite sector (KSAT 2011-2021, GFW 2021-2026) but not in renewable energy PPAs, energy procurement, or the utility/developer ecosystem that LevelTen serves. His infrastructure work was water/wastewater utility security (PA Consulting) and satellite monitoring, not renewable energy transactions.

blockers.type: Hard requirement: Significant commercial/sales experience selling to energy buyers, developers, utilities, advisors
blockers.detail: JD requires 'Significant commercial or sales experience within this market, you have personally sold to or managed relationships with energy buyers, developers, utilities, or advisors and understand how deals get done.' Paul's sales experience is in satellite monitoring services to oil & gas, fishing surveillance, and maritime clients. He has not sold to renewable energy buyers, PPAs, or clean energy developers.

ranked_opportunities.verdict: This is a carbon credit sales role at an Asia-focused carbon project platform. While the mission aligns strongly with Paul's climate/sustainability focus and the entrepreneurial framing is appealing, the core work is enterprise carbon credit sales ,  managing pipeline, closing deals, forecasting ,  not building a commercial function or creating demand in a new market. The JD explicitly requires "7-10 years of experience in enterprise sales, business development, strategic account management" and describes the role as "responsible for the entire carbon credits sales cycle." Paul's KSAT and GFW experience demonstrates commercial development and multi-stakeholder deal architecture, but he is not a quota-carrying enterprise seller. RF capped at 5 because the role shape does not match Paul's market-builder identity. IL at 5 because Paul has relevant carbon/climate sector knowledge (GFW commercial licensing, KSAT EO for environmental compliance) but lacks the named "carbon market sales experience strongly preferred." SA scores 6 because Rubicon is described as expanding and the role is "newly added due to business expansion," but the role itself is about selling an existing product (carbon credits) to enterprises, not building the commercial function. SPM scores 7 because multi-stakeholder carbon credit deals and navigating complex corporate buyers fits Paul's pattern, even though the role itself is misaligned. Singapore preference is a soft geographic blocker.

blockers.detail: Role has 'strong preference for candidates based in Singapore.' Paul is based in Taos, NM. While location is described as 'flexible,' the preference signal creates friction.
Role is 'entire carbon credits sales cycle' including 'managing pipeline, executing account strategies' ,  this is quota-carrying enterprise sales, not market-building.

The JD also requires "deep expertise in wholesale power markets, capacity markets, utility procurement, and energy storage," and prefers $500M+ cumulative transactions, neither of which Paul's profile supports. These are hard gaps in named sector experience and domain expertise. The role's shape is attractive (new-build go-to-market, shaping regulation, multi-stakeholder deals), earning SA 7 and SPM 6, but the hard requirement caps hold RF at 4 and IL at 3. The weighted total (~48) places this in Tier 3.

blockers.detail: Role requires 15+ years in energy markets, power development, utility commercial roles, or infrastructure finance. Paul has 15 years in satellite-based commercial services (KSAT 2011-2021, GFW 2021-2026), not energy/utility sector.

JD requires deep expertise in wholesale power markets, capacity markets, utility procurement, and energy storage or flexible resource economics. Paul's expertise is in satellite data services, maritime monitoring, and IUU fishing surveillance.

First, geographic: Singapore-based with strong preference for local candidates; Paul is in Taos and Singapore relocation is a substantial ask. Second, the JD explicitly requires "10, 15+ years of relevant client-facing deal making experience with 5-7 years+ in carbon markets, climate finance, project investing" ,  Paul has 5 years total in the climate/carbon space (GFW 2021-2026), not 5-7+ in carbon markets specifically. Third, the role demands "deep expertise in carbon markets, including project pathways, quality guardrails, offtake structures, supply and demand dynamics" and "significant experience originating, structuring, negotiating, and executing complex multi-million dollar commercial transactions, with a focus on environmental commodity transactions." Paul built a commercial licensing program at GFW; he did not invest in carbon projects, structure offtakes, or negotiate carbon credit purchase agreements. RF capped at 5 (role involves deal-making and multi-stakeholder coordination but in a domain Paul lacks depth), IL capped at 4 (experience gap is explicit and quantified), GC capped at 2 (Singapore residency preference is explicit). SPM scores 8 because the multi-stakeholder deal architecture, portfolio construction, and market-building patterns are strongly present, but that does not overcome the hard blockers.

blockers.detail: Role based in Singapore with strong preference for Singapore-based candidates. Paul is based in Taos, NM and while open to relocation, Singapore represents a significant geographic and cultural distance.

Requires 10-15+ years relevant client-facing deal making experience with 5-7 years+ specifically in carbon markets, climate finance, project investing, infrastructure, commodities, or structured finance. Paul has 5 years at GFW (2021-2026) in carbon/climate but lacks the requisite depth in carbon project investment, carbon credit offtakes, or carbon market structuring.

Requires deep expertise in carbon markets including project pathways, quality guardrails, offtake structures, supply and demand dynamics, delivery and issuance risk. Paul's carbon market experience is limited to building GFW's commercial licensing program, not investing in or structuring carbon credit projects.

ranked_opportunities.role: INTERNATIONAL BUSINESS DEVELOPMENT AND SALES MANAGER VACANCY
ranked_opportunities.verdict: This is a solid commercial development role in Earth observation with genuine market-building elements (APAC, LATAM, Middle East, Africa expansion), which aligns well with Paul's EO sector experience and pattern of opening new markets. However, the role appears to be primarily managing established product lines in new geographies rather than building a commercial function from scratch, the company already has technologies and institutional relationships, and the hire is tasked with 'accelerating' and 'expanding' rather than creating. The Bilbao-based location with no stated remote option is a significant practical consideration requiring international relocation to Spain. RF scores 6 because the role fits Paul's commercial builder profile and EO sector background, but the JD emphasizes 'strategic sales, account management' experience over pure market-creation, and there's no evidence this is a first commercial hire. SA scores 5 because the role involves genuine new-build work in new geographies, but it's expansion of an established capability, not standing up a function from zero. IL scores 5 because Paul's 15 years in EO and government/institutional sales are strong, but the JD's emphasis on 'aerospace, space, Earth observation' sector experience as what will 'help you stand out' (not required) suggests they may prefer candidates with deeper space-hardware or institutional-space backgrounds than Paul's commercial-monitoring focus. SPM scores 7 because selling Earth observation to governments and institutions in emerging markets involves multi-stakeholder coordination and some regulatory shaping, which is adjacent to Paul's patterns. GC scores 3 due to the Bilbao location requirement with no remote flexibility stated.

blockers.detail: Role is based in Bilbao, Spain. Paul is currently in Taos, NM and would need to relocate internationally. This requires consideration of visa/work authorization for Spain and significant relocation.

ranked_opportunities.company: The Allen Institute for Artificial Intelligence
ranked_opportunities.verdict: This role is a hard pass due to a critical hard requirement gap: the JD explicitly requires "5+ years managing teams of 5+ people, preferably geographically diverse and remote." Paul's 15-year career arc at PA Consulting, KSAT, and GFW shows him as an individual contributor commercial builder and BD strategist, not a line manager of teams. The ABCD notes and career intelligence file document zero people-management experience. This is a genuine hard blocker, the role's core responsibility is "Partnerships team manager driving day-to-day priorities, mentors team members, unblocks issues." RF and IL are both capped at 4 under the hard-gap rule. SPM scores 8 because Skylight's situation (building adoption for an AI-driven ocean-protection platform with governments and NGOs in 95+ countries, shaping compliance and enforcement precedent) is a near-textbook match to Paul's pilot-to-precedent and multi-stakeholder patterns, but that pattern match does not overcome the management gap. The SA/SPM cap rule applies: with a hard blocker present and RF/IL both at 4, SA is capped at 7 and SPM at 6, but SPM legitimately reaches 8 based on the problem shape, so I hold SA at 6 (the role itself is running an established program, not building it from scratch, Skylight already serves 95+ countries). Weighted score ~48, sub-Tier-3, not actionable.

blockers.detail: JD requires '5+ years managing teams of 5+ people, preferably geographically diverse and remote.' Paul has no documented people-management experience; his career arc shows individual contributor commercial development and BD roles, not line management of teams.

Role is remote initially but requires willingness to relocate to Seattle when/if the 12-month term converts to full-time. Paul will relocate anywhere for the right role, so this is informational only.

ranked_opportunities.role: Director of Commercial Business Development (all genders)
ranked_opportunities.verdict: Reflex Aerospace is a post-2021 NewSpace startup building microsatellites at speed, a genuine commercial inflection point. The role itself combines hands-on BD (pipeline ownership, deal closing) with market strategy development across European institutional and commercial customers, which maps well to Paul's market-builder profile. However, two soft gaps cap the scores: the JD explicitly requires "7+ years of experience in sales, business development, or programme acquisition within the space, aerospace or another deep-tech sector" and "solid technical understanding of space systems and their applications." Paul has 15 years in the space sector but in satellite data services (EO analytics, monitoring, vessel detection), not spacecraft platform sales or satellite manufacturing. His technical literacy is in data products (SAR, AIS, imagery), not spacecraft subsystems or mission design. These are sector-depth gaps within space, not hard blockers, Paul's commercial-building patterns transfer, and his institutional/ESA ecosystem knowledge is relevant, but they justify RF capped at 5 and IL capped at 4. SA scores 6 (the role has new-build elements, "next stage of growth," "build a world-class commercial team", but much of the work is scaling an existing motion). SPM 6 reflects genuine multi-stakeholder complexity (ESA, primes, national agencies, commercial operators) but not Paul's three specific patterns. GC 5 reflects Berlin relocation requirement (hybrid, but based in Germany). A stretch role worth surfacing, but the spacecraft-platform gap is real.

blockers.detail: JD requires '7+ years of experience in sales, business development, or programme acquisition within the space, aerospace or another deep-tech sector.' Paul has 15 years of commercial experience but primarily in satellite data services/EO analytics (KSAT, GFW), not spacecraft platform/satellite manufacturing sales. This is a sector-depth gap within the broader space industry.

Role requires 'solid technical understanding of space systems and their applications' with focus on satellite platform development, microsatellite missions, and spacecraft engineering. Paul's technical literacy is in EO data products (SAR, AIS, vessel detection, imagery analytics), not spacecraft subsystems, mission design, or satellite manufacturing.

watch_list.category: PRIORITY FOLLOW-UP: Wildfire Detection / Thermal Infrared Satellite EO
watch_list.why: Munich-based, US HQ opened April 2025 in Denver (~4 hours from Taos). 180+ team, 40+ nationalities, wildfire-solution product line via SAFIRE Gen4 thermal infrared cameras. Kepler's marquee reference customer for hosted payload + edge compute + optical relay. US team is 4 people focused entirely on business development and initial sales outreach, planning to expand fast. Thomas Grubler (co-founder, global CSO, and President/CEO of OroraTech USA) is running double duty and needs a US commercial builder in Denver. Paul's fit is unusually strong: 10 years KSAT commercial build (including thermal infrared fire detection), 5 years GFW commercial program on top of open-access platform, and current in-process Kepler context gives a warm entry angle through their partnership. Vertical: civil protection, infrastructure and energy, forestry, carbon, climate - all Paul's KSAT and GFW buyer categories.
watch_list.roast: PRIORITY ACTION: LinkedIn outreach to Thomas Grubler (President, OroraTech USA, Denver). Draft note ready in Claude chat July 8. Warm entry via Kepler partnership context (studied their full-stack architecture during four-round Kepler process). Secondary target: Dr. Axel Roenneke, global CCO (Munich, ex-Beyond Gravity, Airbus, Rolls-Royce). If Grubler does not respond within 5-7 business days, escalate to Roenneke. Do NOT use the website's speculative-application form; named-person LinkedIn is stronger for senior commercial roles. Currently only one open role posted (Munich compliance role, not a fit), but they explicitly welcome speculative applications and are hiring on the US commercial side.

watch_list.why: Diversified carbon removal (regen ag, agroforestry, biochar, enhanced weathering), selling through Puro/Isometric/Verra/Gold Standard, just raised $20M Series B, India-based with global ambitions. Commercial licensing and data partnerships fits Paul's exact profile.
watch_list.roast: You are hedging every carbon removal pathway simultaneously, which in 2026 makes you either very smart or very confused. Regenerative agriculture, agroforestry, biochar, enhanced rock weathering, and you sell verified credits through five different registries because you cannot decide which methodology will survive the next wave of investigative journalism. Raised $20M leading into a Series B round in February 2026, which means someone believes the diversification play, or at least believes that one of your four bets will hit and the rest can be written off quietly. Paul has done this exact dance. At GFW he built a commercial licensing program on top of a free platform by identifying the narrow sliver of use cases where someone had to pay for a structured relationship even when the data was technically available for nothing. He knows how to parse real differentiation from noise. Varaha is either building the only carbon platform that will matter when half the current MRV stack goes bust, or you are spreading so thin that you will do nothing well. The question Paul will ask in the first meeting is this: which one of your four pathways has actual signed offtake at a price that covers your MRV cost and still makes the farmer money? If the answer involves the words pipeline or projected, the meeting is over. If you have a spreadsheet and a bank wire, he will listen.

watch_list.why: Amsterdam-based, combines satellite monitoring with field sensors and mobile apps for continuous MRV, integrates socioeconomic and biodiversity metrics alongside carbon. Multi-stakeholder data complexity aligns with Paul's ecosystem deal experience.
watch_list.roast: Amsterdam-based agroforestry MRV using satellites, field sensors, and mobile apps for continuous verification, plus you track socioeconomic and biodiversity co-benefits because apparently carbon alone was not hard enough to sell. This is either the future of credible nature-based solutions or the most expensive way to prove that a tree is still a tree. You are trying to do what everyone in the voluntary carbon market says they want, which is holistic measurement that goes beyond tonnage, but nobody has figured out how to price holistic in a market that barely prices carbon. Paul spent three years at KSAT building an oil spill monitoring service that required satellite operators, weather model providers, government regulators, and oil majors to all agree on what constituted actionable intelligence. Then he did it again at GFW with a three-party Planet deal where the third party originally thought GFW was going to destroy their business. Treecycle is a multi-stakeholder MRV problem disguised as a technology problem. The sensor network is the easy part. The hard part is convincing a corporate buyer that the biodiversity and community impact numbers you are reporting are worth paying extra for when they can buy cheaper credits from someone who only measures carbon and calls it a day. If Paul joins, he will not build you a sales pitch. He will build you a licensing framework that separates the buyers who actually need your full stack from the ones who are just checking a box. And he will tell you in month two if that market actually exists.

watch_list.why: Bremen-based VHR optical constellation, first launch Q4 2026, €5.3M seed, ESA Copernicus contract, dual-use (climate + defense)
watch_list.roast: Bremen, Germany. Five million euros seed. First satellite Q4 2026. Very high resolution optical, up to hourly revisit, climate and defense dual-use. You spent a decade at KSAT selling oil spill monitoring to people who did not want to buy it, then another three years at GFW trying to convince an open-access nonprofit that commercial revenue was not a betrayal. Marble is pre-revenue, pre-launch, and dual-use, which means they are selling a capability nobody has seen yet to buyers in two different regulatory universes. The good news is you have done this before. The KSAT pitch in 2011 was a satellite service that did not exist yet sold into a market that did not believe it needed one. You won Shell and Exxon anyway. Marble has an ESA Copernicus Contributing Missions contract, which is the European equivalent of someone important enough to matter saying yes before anyone else did. They need someone who can walk into a room with a German defense procurement officer and a Norwegian environmental regulator on the same day and make the same satellite sound like the answer to completely different questions. That is the whole job. The risk is they are heavily EU-funded in a geopolitical moment when EU defense budgets are finally moving but commercial EO margins are not, and if they cannot cross the valley between those two revenue streams in the next eighteen months the runway ends. You would be building the commercial function in parallel with the first satellite going up, which is either perfect timing or twelve months too early depending on whether the imagery is any good. No one knows yet. The founders came out of the German satellite industry, not from a sales background, so they have built the thing and now need someone to figure out who pays for it and why. If you can figure that out before the money runs out, this is the kind of role where you get to define the category. If you cannot, it is a very expensive science project. Either way, it is exactly the kind of too-early market-creation problem you have spent twenty years not walking away from.

watch_list.category: Thermal Imaging EO / Urban Climate Intelligence
watch_list.why: India-based thermal IR constellation, $5.5M raised, urban heat island pilots (Ahmedabad, Tumakuru), climate resilience + agriculture applications
watch_list.roast: Bangalore, India. Five and a half million raised. Thermal infrared constellation. Urban heat islands, air pollution monitoring, climate resilience at scale. They have pilots running in Ahmedabad and Tumakuru covering four hundred thousand people, and they have forty-two million in letters of intent, which means someone is interested enough to sign something but not interested enough to wire money yet. You know that gap. You lived in it for two years at KSAT waiting for Shell to move from maybe to yes. The thermal piece is interesting because it is not another visible-light me-too play, and the climate application is directly in your lane, but this is an India-based startup selling into Indian government and enterprise buyers, which is a market you have never worked and a procurement environment that makes NOPSEMA look transparent. The opportunity is that thermal intelligence for urban climate adaptation is a real and growing need, and if they can prove the model in India the export case writes itself, but the risk is that you would be trying to build a global commercial function for a company whose early traction is entirely domestic and whose go-to-market motion you have no pattern-matching for. They need someone who understands how to sell satellite-derived climate data to cities, which you have done, and who can build the case for why thermal is worth paying for when optical is mostly free, which is a harder sell. The honest question is whether you want to learn a new market from scratch or whether you want to go somewhere your KSAT and GFW networks and regulatory pattern-matching actually apply from day one. If the answer is the former, and you are comfortable with the India market risk and the fact that their revenue is still mostly Letters of Intent, this is a genuine climate mission with differentiated technology. If the answer is the latter, there are plays closer to home.

watch_list.why: Boston-based, $175M raised, DeepSky AI-native weather satellite constellation, insurance and enterprise weather intelligence platform
watch_list.roast: Tomorrow.io just raised another $35 million to launch its DeepSky satellite constellation and expand AI-driven weather intelligence, which means they are still burning venture capital to out-forecast NOAA while convincing enterprise customers that proprietary weather models are worth the subscription. They have been at this for years. The pitch is always the same. Better forecasts, faster updates, hyperlocal precision for logistics, energy, aviation, insurance. The buyers are real. Delta, Uber, JetBlue. The use case is obvious. Weather drives billions in operational cost and nobody trusts the free forecast when money is on the table. You know this market. You sold monitoring services to industries that could not afford to guess wrong. The difference is that you were selling evidence, not predictions. ICEYE sells what happened. Tomorrow.io sells what might happen. The edge case for you is partnerships and data licensing. They are launching satellites, which means they will need to explain to customers why they should pay for Tomorrow.io radar plus Tomorrow.io satellite plus Tomorrow.io models instead of stitching free sources together. That explanation is a commercial argument, not a features list. You have made that argument in three different industries. If they have a partnerships role open and you can stomach selling probabilistic intelligence instead of deterministic proof, the pitch writes itself. The risk is that weather is a features war and every model is an API call away from commodification. You would be walking into that fight on purpose.

watch_list.category: Very Low Earth Orbit Optical EO
watch_list.why: 10cm resolution from 200-300km VLEO orbit, first launch 2026, highest-res commercial optical imagery on the planet
watch_list.roast: You spent a decade at KSAT watching customers pay Maxar and DigitalGlobe eye-watering rates for sub-meter imagery because the use case demanded it and no one else could deliver. Albedo is betting they can undercut that entire value chain by flying satellites so low they burn through fuel like a Tromsø winter burns through diesel, achieving ten-centimeter resolution with commercially available optics instead of billion-dollar government glass. First satellite launches this year, and if the orbit holds and the optics deliver, they will own the defense and critical infrastructure surveillance market overnight. The technical risk is huge. The regulatory risk is bigger. But you negotiated Shell and ExxonMobil into proactive monitoring contracts when the entire industry said it was impossible, so you know exactly what happens when a new capability crosses the threshold from science project to something a procurement officer can no longer afford to ignore. Mountain View, sixty million raised, and a pitch that sounds insane until you remember that Planet flew cubesats when everyone said imagery required school bus sized satellites. You have spent twenty years on both sides of the EO licensing table. You know which conversations matter. This one matters.

watch_list.why: Vertically integrated platform delivering turnkey climate data products (GHG monitoring, methane detection, wildfire risk), not raw imagery
watch_list.roast: Most EO companies are still trying to sell raw imagery to customers who have no idea what to do with a ten-terabyte TIFF file and a vague promise that insights exist somewhere inside it. Muon Space looked at that model and said no thanks, we will build the entire stack, fly the sensors ourselves, run the processing, and deliver the actual product the customer needs, which is a number they can put in a spreadsheet or a polygon they can show their board. Methane leak detection, wildfire risk scores, greenhouse gas monitoring. The pitch is that they are selling intelligence, not data, which is the only way this market ever scales beyond a few hundred technical buyers. You built GFW commercial licensing by identifying where derived products had private sector value even when the underlying data was free. Muon is doing the same thing but backwards, keeping the data closed and productizing the outputs. They have raised serious money, they are flying real hardware, and their customer list includes exactly the types of organizations that have budget authority and no patience for data engineering projects. The challenge is the same one you hit at GFW, which is that the pipeline narrows fast once you move past the early adopters. But if anyone can take a climate data product into insurance, agriculture, and financial risk modeling, it is someone who already did it once with fishing vessels and knows where the commercial conversation lives.

watch_list.why: AI-based hyperspectral imaging for floods, wildfires, ecosystem detection. Singapore-based, EO domain fit.
watch_list.roast: Singapore hyperspectral startup doing flood and wildfire detection from orbit. You spent a decade at KSAT watching every polar-orbiting EO satellite of relevance fly overhead while you built oil spill monitoring into a multi-vertical portfolio, so you know exactly what hyperspectral can and cannot do. RIFFAI is selling AI-driven ecosystem shift detection to a market that still thinks Landsat 8 is cutting edge. The technology is real, the operational question is whether their buyers understand the difference between spectral signature and actual ground truth, because you learned that lesson in 2013 when ExxonMobil told you poor performance in many areas. The pitch is climate adaptation intelligence, the business model is enterprise SaaS to governments and insurance, and the org is young enough that they probably do not have a commercial function yet. You would be the one building it. Check the careers page, ask who their first five customers were, and if they say UN or AWS partnership without naming a paying contract, you know the distance between demo and revenue.

watch_list.why: Accelerated mineral weathering with patented MRV sensors. Ocean-based CDR, fits maritime and carbon background.
watch_list.roast: Enhanced weathering startup out of the US doing ocean alkalinity measurement with patented CDR and MRV sensors in real time. They are combining mineral weathering, carbon chemistry monitoring, and integration with existing coastal infrastructure, which sounds like someone read the same enhanced weathering papers you did and decided to productize the sensor layer instead of the removal itself. You negotiated a 1.2 billion hectare Planet deal and built an ASN subsea cable partnership from a cold call, so you understand the three-party deal structure this requires. The coastal infrastructure play is smart because it avoids the we need to build everything from scratch trap that kills ocean CDR companies. The MRV sensor is the differentiated asset. The commercial question is whether they are selling carbon credits, selling MRV as a service to other removal projects, or licensing the sensor tech, because those are three different businesses. If the answer is all three, they have not decided yet, which means you would be the one deciding. That is either the perfect builder role or a signal that they have technology without a business model. Poke around, see if they have offtake, and if the CEO has a PhD and no commercial experience, you have seen this movie before.

watch_list.why: Carbon credit integrity ratings. SaaS platform for buyers, investors, traders. Fits GFW commercial licensing and market-making pattern.
watch_list.roast: Carbon credit ratings platform telling buyers which offsets are real and which are marketing. You built a commercial licensing program on top of a free platform at GFW by identifying where the open-access product creates demand for a verified commercial relationship, so you know exactly what Calyx is doing. They are the S&P for voluntary carbon markets, except the market is a dumpster fire and everyone knows it, which is why a ratings business exists at all. The SaaS model is sound, the customer base is buyers and brokers who got burned once and need someone else to do diligence, and the timing is decent because CORSIA Phase 1 is starting to force real money into the market. The question is whether corporate buyers trust a third-party rater or whether they just stop buying credits entirely, which is the actual trend. You would be selling credibility as a product, which is either the highest-margin business in climate tech or the hardest, depending on whether the voluntary carbon market survives the next eighteen months. If they have fifty enterprise customers and recurring revenue, take the call. If they are still venture-funded with no clear path to breakeven, it is a bet on market structure that might not materialize. You know how to evaluate that, you did it with GFW insurance deals under a Moore Foundation pilot when no one knew if the channel would exist.

watch_list.why: Carbon Direct just acquired them (Nov 2025). AI + satellite forest carbon MRV at scale, Verra DMRV pilot partner, direct GFW analog on the commercial licensing paradox.
watch_list.roast: Pachama built the forest carbon MRV stack everyone said was impossible, then got bought by Carbon Direct before they could really figure out whether being a platform or a marketplace was the actual business. You spent a decade at GFW navigating the open data paradox, Paul. Pachama had the opposite problem: they had the tech, the Verra pilot, the satellite + AI credibility, and buyers who actually wanted to pay, but they still ended up inside someone else's go to market engine. That should tell you something. The MRV layer is critical infrastructure but infrastructure rarely wins as a standalone play unless you own distribution, and Pachama didn't. What they did own: four years of training data nobody else has, relationships with every serious forest carbon project developer in the voluntary market, and the only DMRV framework Verra actually trusts. Carbon Direct gets the platform, but they also inherited a team that knows how to talk to both sides of a three party negotiation. You closed Planet at 95 percent off because you understood competitive harm wasn't about price, it was about latency and use case separation. Pachama's version of that insight is that MRV isn't a product, it is the trust layer that makes a market. If you can articulate why continuous satellite monitoring replaces episodic site visits without saying the words "transparency" or "integrity" like a consultant, you should be talking to whoever runs partnerships there now. They need someone who has actually sold data derived from free platforms into commercial contexts and lived.

watch_list.why: No-code satellite data platform purpose-built for NbS project integrity and digital MRV. Paul's buyer profile: non-technical project developers who need EO analysis without hiring a data scientist.
watch_list.roast: Earth Blox is what happens when someone finally admits that most carbon project developers cannot code and do not want to learn Python just to run a Sentinel 2 NDVI time series. They built a no code EO analytics platform aimed directly at the MRV trust problem in nature based solutions, which sounds like product market fit until you remember that the voluntary carbon market spent 2024 and 2025 in an existential crisis and most buyers still do not know whether to pay for practice based credits or satellite verified ones. You have spent fifteen years explaining why EO data is worth paying for to people who were not sure they needed it yet, Paul. Earth Blox has the same problem but in a market with even less commercial maturity. What they do have: former PwC UK Nature Analytics lead as co founder, actual traction with NbS project developers, and positioning at the exact moment compliance markets like Singapore and Brazil are demanding digital MRV that is both rigorous and scalable. If you can walk into a room and explain how a no code EO tool saves a project developer six months and forty thousand dollars on third party verification without over promising what satellites can actually measure at field scale, you might be the first person they have talked to who actually gets it. The company is UK based but the market they are building is global and the sales motion is ecosystem driven, not transactional. You built GFW's commercial function by identifying where derived data products have value that the underlying open data does not. Earth Blox's version of that is: the analysis is the product, not the imagery. If you can sell that story without making it sound like SaaS vaporware, you should.

watch_list.why: Satellite MRV for soil carbon and regen ag. Major player in ag carbon space, $3.2B market growing 12.8% CAGR. Buyer ecosystem includes Planet, Corteva, food companies.
watch_list.roast: Regrow Ag is in the soil carbon MRV business, which means they are selling the promise that satellites and models can tell you how much carbon is in dirt you cannot see, on farms you will never visit, to buyers who are one news cycle away from deciding the whole thing is a scam. The ag carbon market is theoretically enormous and actually a minefield. You know this, Paul. You spent two years at KSAT convincing oil majors that synthetic aperture radar could find their spills before Greenpeace did, which required both technical credibility and the commercial argument that reputational risk is quantifiable. Regrow's version of that pitch is: regenerative agriculture sequesters carbon, we can measure it at scale with satellite imagery and modeling, and food companies will pay for verified credits to meet scope three targets. All of that is true until someone asks how much ground truthing you actually did and whether practice based estimates are just expensive guessing. What Regrow has going for it: Planet Labs and Corteva money, an MRV platform that integrates optical and radar, and positioning in a three billion dollar market that is growing fast because compliance is coming whether the tech is ready or not. The CBAM and EPA methane mandates are real regulatory forcing functions. If you can sell a complex multi provider EO ecosystem to a conservative enterprise buyer and make the ROI case before the pilot ends, Regrow needs that. They have the tech, they have the funding, but the commercial path from agronomic SaaS to carbon credit infrastructure is not obvious and nobody there has built a licensing program on top of a platform that was designed to give the data away for free. You have.

watch_list.why: Extreme weather forecasting for insurance, utilities, emergency response using satellite and radar
watch_list.roast: Climavision is building radar and satellite weather forecasting for the people who will actually pay when a forecast matters. Not consumer weather apps with animated raindrops. Not free NOAA wrappers with venture cosmetics. Insurance carriers pricing parametric risk. Utilities trying not to burn California again. Emergency managers who need thirty minutes, not thirty hours. You spent a decade at KSAT selling oil spill monitoring to Shell when they realized not knowing was more expensive than knowing. Same structural move here. The buyer already has weather data. What they do not have is the system that tells them which forecast to trust when the models diverge and the claim is seven figures. Climavision sells decision confidence, not data feeds. The company is early enough that a partnerships role would mean building the commercial function around a technical capability that works but has not yet found all its revenue channels. You have done that twice. Once in Tromso when KSAT had satellites and no customers. Once at GFW when you had to convince the board that commercial licensing was not mission drift. If you can explain to an actuary why radar beats satellite at convective nowcasting and to a satellite operator why their L-band data completes the radar picture, you are the translator they need. The risk is that everyone thinks they can build a weather company until they meet their first enterprise buyer and realize weather is infrastructure, not features. You know the difference. That is the edge.

watch_list.category: Climate Intelligence / Satellite Data for Agriculture
watch_list.why: Israel-based,sat data + climate models for land value chains, fits EO and climate mission overlap
watch_list.roast: Marvin is an Israeli startup using satellite imagery, climate models, and AI to help agricultural value chains figure out what is actually happening on the ground before the crop fails or the supply chain snaps. They are not selling dashboards to sustainability teams. They are selling operational intelligence to buyers who lose money when the forecast is wrong. Food companies trying to lock long-term supply in a world where reliability is collapsing. Insurers repricing parametric ag coverage because loss ratios just broke their models. Governments trying to manage subsidy programs without field inspectors. You have spent two decades explaining to skeptical customers why paying for earth observation beats guessing. You sold Shell on proactive monitoring when their default was reactive cleanup. You sold GFW funders on why satellite AIS was worth the price when broadcast AIS was free. The Marvin pitch is structurally identical. The buyer has Sentinel data they do not use, agronomists who cannot scale, and supply risk they cannot quantify. What they need is someone who understands both the technical stack and the commercial argument well enough to make the pilot feel inevitable. The company is small, probably ten to twenty people, venture-backed, still defining the revenue model. A partnerships or BD role there is not joining a machine. It is building the machine. You like that part. The problem is always the same. Can a climate data company that should sell to everyone focus long enough to dominate one vertical before the funding runs out. If you join, your job is to answer that question faster than they burn cash.

watch_list.why: Promoted to Tier A proactive target 2026-07-06: >EUR1B round closed Jun 2026 at >EUR10B valuation, 2025 revenue >EUR250M with positive EBITDA, EUR1.5B backlog, ~$2B German national SAR network. ICEYE US (Irvine) is a real entity, so a US seat carries no foreign flag. SAR services = Paul's literal KSAT decade; outreach via insurance and allied-government verticals where clearances do not gate.
watch_list.roast: ICEYE is a Finnish SAR satellite company that just locked a $350 million credit facility from a seven-bank syndicate, which tells you two things. One, they have revenue. Two, the revenue is probably coming from defense and intelligence customers who pay on time and do not negotiate. They have been flying SAR smallsats since 2018, building a constellation for persistent monitoring, and selling radar imagery and intelligence products to governments, insurers, and maritime customers. This is your lane. You spent a decade at KSAT explaining to oil majors why radar does not care about clouds and to government buyers why revisit matters more than resolution. You know what it takes to move a SAR capability from a tech demo into a service contract that renews. ICEYE has done that at scale. They have customers. They have satellites. They have a business model that works. What they probably do not have is someone who understands both the satellite operator perspective and the buyer perspective well enough to build partnerships that are not just data resale. You negotiated Planet imagery at a ninety-five percent discount by understanding what each party actually needed. You built the ASN subsea cable partnership by recognizing a non-obvious application for vessel tracking data. ICEYE needs that skill set. The company is past the startup romance phase. This is industrial execution. Deliver the tasking, close the contract, scale the revenue. If you are looking for a mission-driven save-the-world vibe, this is not it. If you are looking for a commercial role where your EO and maritime domain experience converts directly into leverage, start the conversation.

watch_list.why: YC-backed, uses satellite imagery + operational data for water risk assessment, enterprise SaaS selling to Coke/Diageo/Colgate, direct analog to GFW enterprise licensing model
watch_list.roast: YC-backed water risk platform that combines satellite imagery with corporate operational data to tell Coca-Cola and Diageo exactly how screwed their supply chains are when the aquifer runs dry, which is a pitch that works because nobody wants to be the CFO explaining why the bottling plant went offline because you didn't see the drought coming. They have Coke, Colgate, and Ab InBev as customers, which means somebody in procurement has already blessed a contract for something that sounds like science, and you have spent your entire career being the person who turns 'sounds like science' into 'renews automatically because we can't live without it'. The model is enterprise SaaS built on satellite data, which is the exact structural problem you solved at GFW when you figured out that insurance companies and maritime risk buyers will pay for derived data products even when the underlying platform is open access, and Waterplan is the same play but for water instead of fishing. The company is early enough that a commercial hire would be building rather than inheriting, but mature enough that they have a functioning sales motion and a customer list you can point to, which is the Goldilocks zone for someone who wants to own the function but not have to invent the category from scratch. The founders are second-time operators with exits, the investors include Leonardo DiCaprio and Richard Branson's family, which tells you the cap table has people who understand narrative and also that this is the kind of climate company that can get press when it needs to. The product is legitimately useful, the buyer is obvious, and the sales cycle is long enough that your KSAT patience and GFW stakeholder choreography would be the differentiator. If they are looking for someone to scale partnerships or build a licensing arm for the imagery and risk data, you are the person who has done that in two adjacent markets already.

watch_list.why: Thermal EO constellation, recently back in business after funding, defense and commercial applications, unique dataset with licensing potential
watch_list.roast: British thermal imaging satellite company that went quiet for a bit and is now officially back in business, which in the EO world usually means they either found a cornerstone customer who wrote a big enough check to make the unit economics work or they convinced a new set of investors that thermal intelligence is finally a category and not just a science experiment. Thermal EO is one of those datasets that everyone agrees is incredibly valuable right up until the moment you ask them to pay for it, and SatVu has been grinding through that exact conversation for years, so if they are hiring now it means someone finally said yes. The use cases are real, the defense applications are obvious, and the commercial verticals are insurance, energy infrastructure, and anyone else who needs to know what is hot when optical imagery can't see it, which is a narrow enough market that you have to be good at finding the buyers who will pay and patient enough to build the deals one stakeholder at a time. You have done that twice. KSAT built the commercial monitoring business in oil and gas when nobody was buying, GFW built the licensing function when the organization didn't believe commercial revenue was legitimate, and SatVu would be the same pattern but with a dataset that is genuinely scarce and a buyer base that skews government and enterprise. The company is not a startup anymore, they have flown hardware, and if they are scaling the commercial side that means they need someone who can structure partnerships, build licensing frameworks, and translate a unique technical capability into a repeatable revenue motion. You are that operator. The question is whether they are hiring for it or just trying to keep the lights on, but if it is the former, you should be in the conversation.

watch_list.why: AI-driven satellite imagery interpretation for climate impact, natural resource monitoring, ag risk. Singapore-based, direct fit for Paul's EO + climate buyer expertise.
watch_list.roast: Singapore outfit using AI to interpret satellite imagery for climate insights. Which is what everyone with a GPU and a Planet API key has been doing since 2019, but sure, call it a startup. The pitch deck probably has a slide about how nobody else thought to point machine learning at Sentinel-2 data. You spent a decade at KSAT stitching together multi-provider EO ecosystems and teaching oil majors that proactive monitoring beats reactive crisis management. You know the difference between a technology demo and a business model. LambdAI's natural resource monitoring sounds like every other remote sensing consultancy that discovered AWS SageMaker last year. But if they have actual recurring revenue from someone who isn't their seed investor's portfolio company, and if they need someone who can explain to a procurement officer at an ag insurer why paying for this beats free Copernicus data, you can make that call in your sleep. The question is whether they have anyone in the room who has actually closed a deal with someone who wasn't already a believer. That's where you'd walk in. Check the careers page. If it's all ML engineers and zero commercial hires, you already know the problem.

watch_list.why: LiDAR-equipped satellite constellation for continuous GHG emissions monitoring. Germany-based. Methane detection, CO2 tracking, exactly the regulatory-commercial intersection Paul knows from ENI/NOPSEMA and Planet methane work.
watch_list.roast: German startup building a LiDAR satellite constellation to track methane and CO2 in near-real-time. Spectrometers in space pointing at oil fields and telling you what you already knew but couldn't prove in a boardroom. The pitch is continuous monitoring with granularity that makes current reporting look like self-assessment theater. Which it is. You negotiated the Planet Labs deal that put 200 million hectares a day through a detection pipeline. You know exactly what the gap is between a beautiful map of a methane plume and a contract that someone's compliance officer will actually sign. AIRMO is solving a real problem but they're launching into a market where half the potential buyers are waiting to see if the regulations survive the next election cycle and the other half are trying to figure out if satellite-detected emissions create legal liability they don't currently have. If they need someone who can walk into a room with an energy company's environmental lead and say we both know you're emitting more than you're reporting, let's figure out the commercial structure that makes transparency feel less like a threat, that's a conversation you've had in five languages across three continents. The founder is probably a PhD who has never sold anything that wasn't a grant application. You'd fix that in six months.

watch_list.why: YC-backed digital MRV for EU forest carbon projects, addressing climate-vulnerable forests with tech stack
watch_list.roast: YC-backed German outfit building digital tools for forest carbon certification across the EU, which sounds great until you remember that European forests are burning at rates no one predicted three years ago and the voluntary carbon market has spent 2025 explaining why half its credits were fake. Pina Earth is betting they can monitor and certify projects fast enough to matter, using satellite data and ground sensors to automate what used to require manual field visits and prayer. The problem they are solving is real. EU forests are high-risk, landowners need revenue to adapt, and carbon finance could pay for resilience if anyone still trusts the credits. You spent a decade at KSAT watching governments write check after check for satellite monitoring they never quite deployed at scale, then built GFW's licensing program by finding the buyers who could not afford to not know. Pina Earth is that same structural bet but aimed at a European carbon market that does not exist yet in compliance form and a voluntary market that got kneecapped by journalism in 2023. If they survive until the EU compliance framework lands, they own the certification layer for every forester in Bavaria. If the market stays broken, they are selling software no one wants to buy. Either way, they need someone who has sold satellite-derived environmental data to people who were not sure they needed it yet, which is uncomfortably close to your entire professional biography. Check the careers page, but this one might hurt.

watch_list.why: Advanced chemical DAC with basalt mineralization partnerships, addresses scalability and permanence
watch_list.roast: Direct air capture startup building machines that pull CO2 out of the atmosphere using chemical filters, then partner with someone else to inject it into basalt for permanent mineralization. The tech is conceptually sound. Draw air through a selective adsorbent, heat the filters with steam to release concentrated CO2, hand it off to a geology partner who pumps it underground where it turns into rock. Permanent, verifiable, scalable in theory. The business model is harder. DAC has been the supermodel of climate tech for five years. Everyone wants to fund it, no one wants to be the first corporate offtake customer at $600 per tonne when they can buy forestry credits for $15 and hope no one checks the methodology too closely. You watched this movie at GFW when you built the commercial licensing program on top of a free platform and had to argue internally that someone would pay for data they could get for nothing if you repositioned it correctly. Sirona has the same problem but worse, because their customer is not buying imagery or vessel tracking, they are buying tonnes of removed CO2 at a price that makes their CFO cry. The wedge is finding the buyer who cares more about the permanence guarantee and the press release than the cost per tonne, and that is a short list. If they have traction with industrial buyers who need hard removal for compliance or corporate commitments, this could be real. If they are still living off grants and pre-purchase agreements from philanthropies, they are two years from figuring out the commercial motion and you would be building it from scratch. You have done that twice. You know the scar tissue it leaves. If you are ready to do it again in a harder market with a product that costs 40x more than the substitutes, send the application. If you want to sleep at night, find a company with revenue.

watch_list.why: Top proactive target 2026-07-06: freshly public (NYSE HAWK, May 2026), 4th straight US Navy Indo-Pacific MDA renewal ($98.8M), $131M India FMS, $75M European MoD; needs allied-gov and commercial MDA growth beyond classified USG work. Cold outreach; the allied-MDA lane needs no clearance.
watch_list.roast: Herndon's RF eavesdroppers went public in May and now have to explain to shareholders that classified revenue, however patriotic, does not compound like a commercial franchise. Their unclassified maritime domain awareness business sells to coast guards and navies who all want to know which dark vessel is lying about its name, which is coincidentally the question Paul spent five years answering for free at Global Fishing Watch. Somebody should tell them the free version came with a sales pipeline attached.

watch_list.why: CORRECTION 2026-07-07: Paul already applied to this exact role (Director, Business Development - Commercial Space) on 2026-04-16 with resume, cover letter, and essay; silent for 80+ days and now auto-aged out of the active view. The angle is a follow-up or a fresh executive-level note about the Series A GTM build, not a new application.
watch_list.roast: A radio company that put its radios on other people's satellites and then noticed someone has to sell the airtime. The Series A press release says they are transforming mission-critical space infrastructure, which translates to: we hired engineers for four years and just discovered the org chart has no one in it who likes customers. The posted comp suggests they discovered it with feeling.

watch_list.why: UPDATE 2026-07-07: the live Denver seats are BDM Satellite Sales (scored 67.0, on the board) and BDM Space Hardware (scored 48.5 on 2026-07-07, junior hardware quota seat). No Space Services posting exists; the other BDM seats sit in Toulouse/Munich/Berlin. The play: apply to Satellite Sales and pitch US CEO Rusty Thomas above the title on the wider US commercial build.
watch_list.roast: Sofia's finest satellite factory hired a DARPA veteran to run America and told him to triple Denver. The job ads say Business Development Manager, but the org underneath is three people and a cleanroom, so the first senior person through the door gets to draw the map. Bulgarians building buses, Coloradans selling them; somewhere a procurement officer is very confused about time zones.

watch_list.why: CORRECTION 2026-07-07: Paul applied to this exact Director of Business Development and Partnerships seat on 2026-04-15; acknowledged, then silence. The posting still being live after 80+ days suggests the application did not advance. Angle: a direct note to the new CCO with a sharper wildfire-insurance argument, not a re-apply.
watch_list.roast: The wildfire modeling shop every California utility calls when the wind picks up, now owned by private equity and therefore contractually obligated to grow. They just partnered their way into insurance catastrophe modeling, a market where the buyers are institutionally terrified and the regulation writes the budget, which is the exact weather system Paul has been selling into for twenty years. La Jolla headquarters, US-remote role, no excuses.

watch_list.why: CORRECTION 2026-07-07: no US Government sales director posting exists on Kepler's live Lever board (the listing was a stale aggregator hit). More important: Paul is in a live FINAL ROUND for Kepler's Director of Sales, US Commercial Space (round 4 held June 15). Priority is closing that process; the only live senior alternative is Director of Sales - Europe (foreign, pursue-if-sponsored).
watch_list.roast: Toronto's space internet company finally launched the network it has been describing at conferences since 2016, and now faces the awkward part where someone sells capacity on it. Optical relay is ground segment's cooler younger sibling, and Paul spent a decade at KSAT selling the original. The US government director seat is open; the Canadians would like an American to talk to the Americans.

watch_list.why: $60M Series B (Jan 2026) raised explicitly to scale commercial, civil government, and defense GTM plus MENA/India/LatAm expansion; thermal IR water intelligence. VP Sales seat occupied; pitch the market-development seat under it.
watch_list.roast: They point infrared cameras at farmland and tell you which field is thirsty, which sounds niche until you remember that water is the thing wars and commodity prices are made of. Fresh sixty million specifically earmarked for going to market, a DC address full of institutional buyers, and no senior commercial posting yet: the seat exists, it just has not been written down. Paul's favorite kind of job description.

watch_list.why: US-redomesticated, revenue up 38-80% YoY, Merlin constellation first launch Oct 2026, global sales-force expansion announced May 2026 under SVP Global Sales Jeff Kerridge. Outreach directly to Kerridge; sovereign/international BD needs no clearance.
watch_list.roast: The Argentine constellation that moved to North Carolina, pivoted to defense, and started growing 80 percent a year, proving that sometimes the best product strategy is a change of address. They announced a global sales expansion in May, which in a company this size means the SVP is personally drowning. A well-timed letter about sovereign EO programs might read less like an application and more like a rescue.

watch_list.why: UPDATE 2026-07-07: the International BD Director listing was stale. Live seats are Head of BD, USG Markets (requires active TS/SCI, scored 46.0, not actionable) and BD Director, USG Markets (US Person only, scored 47.5, pass on fit). Both on the board 2026-07-07. Apex stays a watch for a commercial/international seat, not an apply.
watch_list.roast: They sell satellite buses the way Toyota sells Corollas: standard trims, a price list, and a straight face, which in aerospace counts as a radical act. Two mega-rounds in nine months means the factory is scaling faster than the sales org, and the International BD seat is the one desk where a clearance matters less than knowing how allied governments actually buy. Paul has the passport stamps to prove he does.

watch_list.why: $44M Series B (Jun 2025) with Liberty Mutual and Tokio Marine as strategics; $100M+ contracted revenue; insurance vertical opening on top of utility base. Cold outreach: the insurance channel needs its own builder. GFW insurance-vertical story is the direct analog.
watch_list.roast: Mountaintop cameras that spot wildfires before the smoke makes the evening news, sold to utilities that would very much like to stop being named in lawsuits. Two insurance giants just wrote checks into the cap table, which is insurance-speak for please build us a product line. Paul watched an insurance vertical materialize out of one narrow question at GFW; here the question is already capitalized and sitting on the board.

watch_list.why: Constellation going live: 6 satellites launched May 2026, commercial ops targeted Sep 2026; GTM being stood up now under VP Sales Sam Lieff. Exclusion lifted 2026-07-06. Paul's EarthDaily letter is the benchmark in his own library. Vancouver corporate = pursue-if-sponsored caveat only.
watch_list.roast: The UrtheCast estate sale that quietly turned into a real constellation, now weeks from switching on daily scientific-grade imaging of the entire planet. Commercial operations start in September and the sales machine is visibly still in the box, which is either alarming or an invitation depending on whether you are an investor or a market builder with a benchmark cover letter already written. The exclusion list finally forgave them; the timing forgives itself.

watch_list.why: Six hyperspectral Fireflies on orbit, second NRO contract in 2026, raising $80-100M at ~$400M (in talks, May 2026) explicitly to scale commercialization; US entity in El Segundo holds NASA/NRO business. US senior seat plausible; center of gravity Bengaluru.
watch_list.roast: Bengaluru's hyperspectral darlings can tell you the chlorophyll content of a wheat field from orbit, and are now raising their biggest round to figure out who pays for that sentence. Mining, agriculture, insurance: category-building across verticals is the actual job, whatever the eventual title says. The El Segundo office holds the American contracts and, plausibly, a desk for someone who has built this exact business twice.

watch_list.why: Non-profit satellite constellation detecting methane super-emitters. Public data portal just launched. Planet Labs partnership. Direct GFW parallel but carbon-focused.
watch_list.roast: You spent a decade selling KSAT oil spill monitoring to the majors and now Carbon Mapper wants to put those same refineries on a public shame map with 30-meter methane plumes updated daily. It is the most magnificent heel-turn available in the EO space right now. They are a 501c3 backed by philanthropy, Planet Labs is building the birds, JPL designed the sensors, and they just went live in June with a global data portal that names names. The whole model is structured like GFW but for carbon instead of fishing, which means you have already lived the nightmare of explaining to enterprise customers why the free platform they are about to get named on is not actually competitive with their premium intelligence product. The difference is that oil and gas buyers do not care about competitive harm when the EPA or an NGO shows up with a thermal plume and a timestamp. They care about being on the list at all. This is not a sales job, it is a regulatory inevitability job, and those were always the deals you closed when everyone else thought the market did not exist yet. Carbon Mapper has two satellites up, a third planned, and no commercial team. If you can figure out who pays for the margin around the mission, you will have built the thing twice.

watch_list.why: India-based, processing satellite and remote sensing via ML for nature-based credits. Blockchain ledger, real-time reporting. MRV pain point is very real.
watch_list.roast: VANYA is an Indian AI and satellite MRV startup trying to fix the voluntary carbon market by processing remote sensing data through machine learning and recording it on a blockchain, which is two hype cycles in one sentence and somehow still the most credible solution to the MRV disaster that the market has produced. They are targeting nature-based credits, which means forestry projects in countries where ground truth is either inaccessible or politically inconvenient, and they are claiming high precision and transaction transparency, which is exactly what every credit buyer says they want until you put a contract in front of them and they start hedging on the methodology. You built a commercial licensing program on top of an open data platform where the internal argument was whether selling anything at all violated the mission. VANYA has the opposite problem, which is that the market desperately wants what they are selling but does not trust anyone selling it. The voluntary carbon market is a MRV graveyard and you know it. If VANYA has figured out how to automate reporting without cutting corners on validation, and if they can get a major credit registry or a financial institution to bless the output, they have a business. If they are just another AI wrapper on Sentinel-2 with a blockchain PR strategy, they have a deck. Go find out which one it is before someone else does.

morning_briefs.notes: Daily / Morning Brief cron commit landed at 12:30 UTC but no morning_briefs entry was appended to dashboard-data.json (existing pipeline bug, last appended entry was 2026-05-01). This run filled the gap. Digest had 276 postings of which ~225 were aggregator-board URLs (Terra.do, Climatebase, Climate Change Jobs by city, Schmidt Marine listings, Esri portfolio, etc.) that link to listing pages rather than specific JDs and are unscoreable per rubric. After applying applications-dedup, watch_list checks, hard exclusions, title-level filtering for engineering/analyst/IC-SDR/recruiter roles, and the recently-ranked window, 8 specific JDs were dispatched to scoring subagents. Subagents could not fetch JDs from their sandboxed environment (WebFetch denied), so fetching was retried in main context. Sierra Club returned 403 (unreadable). 5 successful fetches plus 2 hard-exclusion title-checks plus 1 already-known-pattern (Bentley) produced 4 new Tier 3 dashboard entries: Bees & Bears Head of Risk Berlin (44.5), Bentley Principal Consultant Remote/GA (47.5), SF Estuary Institute Executive Director Richmond CA (52.0), Active Site Chief of Staff Cambridge MA (43.5). No Tier 1 or Tier 2 entries today, no deep dives triggered.
